answer: Argon2id with parameters: 64MB memory, 3 iterations, 4 parallelism factor.
Refresh token expiration period is 7 days with rotation-on-use policy (new token issued with each use).
The Notification Service runs on port 9200.
Currently running PostgreSQL 15.4, upgraded from version 14.9.
API Gateway throttle limits are 5000 burst and 2000 steady-state requests per second.
Argon2id was chosen over bcrypt because it provides side-channel resistance, which bcrypt cannot offer.
Drizzle was chosen instead of Prisma because it has zero runtime dependencies and provides SQL-like TypeScript syntax.
Both Portfolio and Notification services verify auth tokens via the API Gateway Lambda authorizer, which validates JWT tokens against the JWKS endpoint and injects claims into requests.
Rate limiting is done at the ALB (Application Load Balancer) instead of application code because it stops DDoS attacks before reaching containers, providing a network-level defense.

The chain involves: user request → API Gateway HTTP-API → Lambda authorizer (validates JWT via JWKS, injects role claims) → VPCLink → ALB (rate limiting 1000/min/IP) → Portfolio Service (RBAC middleware checks role hierarchy).

If JWKS endpoint goes down, all downstream services (Portfolio, Notification) cannot validate new tokens and auth fails. Auth Service continues to issue tokens using its local private key. There is a 5-minute TTL grace period in the LRU cache (1000 entries) for cached JWKS.
Real-time portfolio alerting depends on both the Notification Service and gateway WebSocket API. Status: planned for Q3 (not yet implemented).
Yes, the UTC+14 token expiry bug has been fixed by using Unix timestamps instead of Date objects, ensuring consistent timezone handling across all regions.

Implement webhook notifications for auth events by using the /api/v1/notifications/webhooks/register endpoint, storing webhook URLs per tenant, triggering POST requests on auth events (login/logout/MFA), and implementing exponential backoff retry policy with 5 attempts over 24 hours.
Maximum simultaneous database connections across 5 Auth Service instances: 5 instances × 20 connections/instance = 100 total connections.

The connection pool (20/instance) is separate from the Celery memory issue. During large tax-lot processing in Portfolio Service (running on Fargate Spot with Celery/Redis), unbounded Celery memory is the issue being investigated, independent of the database connection pool constraints.

No, Fargate Spot cannot be used for Auth Service because Spot instances carry interrupt risk. Fargate Spot is only for batch workloads (portfolio-recalc, digest) that can tolerate interruption, not always-on services.

Multi-service architecture. Auth Service cannot add an in-memory session cache because other services (Portfolio, Notification) need to validate tokens via JWKS without local state. Shared session state would violate the distributed architecture and create tight coupling.

30-day backup retention ensures RPO (Recovery Point Objective) of 1 hour and RTO (Recovery Time Objective) of 4 hours. Backups enable recovery to any point within 30 days; the hourly backup cadence meets RPO requirements. Replicas in us-west-2 support RTO targets.
The RDS upgrade from 14.9 to 15.4 required changing the connection string to sslmode=verify-full and including the global-bundle.pem certificate for SSL/TLS verification.
The tax_lots table columns are: id, account_id, security_id, acquisition_date, cost_basis, quantity, adjusted, created_at.

MFA affects the login flow by requiring an additional verification step after password validation. If MFA is enabled, the user receives a TOTP challenge; they must provide the correct code before issuing the access token.
10 recovery codes are generated during MFA setup. Each code is single-use and permanently consumed upon use; codes cannot be reused.
Webhook retry policy uses exponential backoff with 5 attempts over 24 hours.

Security posture for credential data: At rest: AES-256-GCM encryption with per-tenant keys. Passwords: Argon2id(64MB/3iter/4par). In transit: mTLS enforced via Envoy (AppMesh), database connections use sslmode=verify-full with global-bundle.pem, JWT RS256 used for token verification (no shared secrets between services).

Full login lifecycle with MFA: 1) User submits credentials to /api/v1/auth/login. 2) Gateway validates request schema. 3) Lambda authorizer validates JWT (N/A for login). 4) Auth Service receives request, validates via ALB rate limiting (1000/min/IP). 5) Service queries PostgreSQL, verifies password with Argon2id. 6) If MFA enabled, generate TOTP challenge and return QR URI. 7) User scans QR, submits TOTP code. 8) Server verifies TOTP against stored secret. 9) Issue RS256 access token (15min) and refresh token (7day rotate-on-use). 10) Return tokens to client.

For a new Billing Service in Rust: Apply naming patterns (handleVerb{Resource}), use RFC7807 Problem Details errors with traceId, implement structured JSON logging (compatible with Pino), authenticate via Lambda authorizer + JWKS validation, use TypeScript-agnostic protocols (JSON/gRPC), deploy via GitHub Actions pipeline with Claude Code review, run on ECS-Fargate in 3AZ, use VPCLink/ALB for routing, apply connection pool constraints per instance, use Argon2id for any password operations, enforce mTLS via Envoy/AppMesh.

1500 req/min from single IP hitting Auth Service: 1) ALB rate limiting (1000/min/IP) drops 500 requests immediately. 2) First 1000 requests reach ALB, ~833 sustained (2000 steady-state / 5000 burst policy). 3) Lambda authorizer processes auth claims. 4) Auth Service processes requests within connection pool (20/instance). 5) Excess queued in ALB target group. 6) After sustained period, remaining requests either queue or timeout.

Systems depending on JWT signing key: 1) Auth Service (issues RS256 tokens using private key). 2) Lambda authorizer (validates tokens via public key from JWKS endpoint /.well-known/jwks.json). 3) Portfolio Service (validates tokens to check claims/roles). 4) Notification Service (validates tokens for authenticated webhooks). 5) JWKS endpoint (serves public key for all services). 6) Refresh token rotation mechanism (issues new tokens). Key rotation: 90-day policy.

Deployment pipeline: 1) Developer commits code to GitHub. 2) GitHub Actions triggers: lint → typecheck → test (real PG via testcontainers) → build → ECR push. 3) Claude Code reviews changes before merge approval. 4) Merge to main triggers deployment job. 5) Build new Fargate task image, push to ECR. 6) Blue-green deployment: new tasks launched, ALB target group switched after health checks pass. 7) Old task group terminated. 8) Zero-downtime transition. Monitoring tracks deployment success. Rollback available via target group revert.
